narrative:
I want to start by saying how sorry I am that your law school application was rejected—it's a tough and disappointing experience, and I know how much effort you've put into your education so far. Based on the information we have, the model used to evaluate applications predicted that you have about a 79% chance of not passing the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application wasn't approved this time. This prediction was made by looking at various aspects of your background and academic performance, comparing them to patterns from other students who have taken the bar exam.

Let me walk you through your profile a bit, so you can see what the model considered: you reported an LSAT score of 30.5, which is on the lower side compared to the average of around 36.7; your first-year grades placed you in the 3rd decile, below the typical average; your third-year grades were in the 2nd decile, also lower than average; your family income is in the middle bracket, which is fairly common; your undergraduate GPA is 3.3, slightly above the average; and you identified as female, White, and planning to study full-time, all of which are demographics seen in many applicants.

When it comes to why the model made this prediction, the most influential factors were your LSAT score and your first-year grades, as they played the biggest roles in pushing the decision toward expecting you might struggle with the bar exam. Right after those, your third-year grades and family income were also significant, contributing in a way that suggested challenges ahead. Then, your undergraduate GPA had a noticeable impact, followed by your racial background, gender, and finally, your plan to study full-time, which had the least influence among these.

In essence, the model saw your LSAT score as a key reason to lean toward a higher risk of failure because it was below average, and it viewed your first-year and third-year grades similarly, as they indicated potential difficulties in keeping up with the demands of law school. Even though your undergraduate GPA was a bit stronger than average, it still added to the overall concern in the model's eyes, likely because it didn't outweigh the other weaker areas. This ranking of influences shows that while all these factors played a part, the ones at the top—like your LSAT and early grades—were the heaviest weights tipping the scale.

It's important to remember that this isn't a judgment of your potential; it's just a snapshot based on data from past students. Looking ahead, there are steps you can take to strengthen your profile for future opportunities, like retaking the LSAT to aim for a higher score, as that's something many students improve with focused preparation. You might also focus on boosting your grades if you're still in school, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups to address any weak areas from your first and third years.

Additionally, while factors like family income and demographics aren't things you can change, you can build skills and experiences that highlight your strengths, such as gaining relevant internships or extracurriculars that demonstrate your commitment to law. I encourage you to reach out to academic advisors or mentors who can help you create a plan tailored to your situation, as many students have turned similar setbacks into successes by addressing these key areas. Remember, this is just one step in your journey, and with determination, you can work toward improving your chances for the next application. You're capable of growth, and I'm rooting for you every step of the way.
